1	# Task Prompt
2	
3	A TripForge Itinerary & Decisioning platform capable of managing offline travel itinerary planning, controlled data ingestion, and explainable model-assisted recommendations through a single-node backend API. The system exposes Authentication and Identity APIs supporting local username login only, account registration, password change/recovery via security questions (no email/SMS), and session/JWT issuance with configurable TTL (access token 30 minutes, refresh token 14 days) plus device registration (max 5 active devices per user) and unusual-location detection based on last-known city string supplied by the client and rate-limited challenge prompts. RBAC and Authorization APIs support role, user, permission-point, and “menu” groupings (treated as logical capability bundles) with API-level authorization and data-scope rules (e.g., an Organizer can access only itineraries they own; an Admin can access all). Itinerary APIs support creating itineraries, adding resources (attractions, lodging, meals, meetings) into day-based time slots, and validating conflicts using stored business hours, closure dates, minimum dwell time, and travel-time matrices maintained locally per city/region; scheduling must reject overlaps and enforce a default minimum buffer of 15 minutes between items. The route planning engine provides optimization suggestions using deterministic heuristics (same-area clustering and shortest-path approximation) and returns a ranked list with explainable reasons; every save creates a versioned revision record with diff metadata and allows exporting a standardized itinerary package and a share token that is valid for 7 days on the same deployment. Data Import/Export APIs support Excel/CSV template download, bulk import with pre-validation and row-level error annotations, deduplication via configurable keys (default: resource name + street line + city), and a rollback window of 10 minutes per import batch. Model Management APIs support registering models, semantic versions, activation status, A/B allocations, and canary rollouts; inference is executed locally via Java-side adapters (PMML/ONNX or custom process) with combined rule-and-model decisioning and an “explainability” payload describing top contributing rules/features and confidence bands.
4	
5	The backend uses Express (TypeScript) to provide resource-oriented APIs and Prisma with MySQL for persistence, ensuring all functionality runs without any external network dependencies and can be deployed as a single Docker container. Core tables include Users (unique username, password_hash with bcrypt, status enum, last_login_at), Devices (user_id, device_fingerprint hash, last_seen_at), Roles, PermissionPoints, RoleBindings, AuditLogs (actor, action, resource_type/id, timestamp, immutable), Itineraries, ItineraryVersions, Resources, ResourceHours/Closures, TravelTimeMatrices, ImportBatches/ImportErrors, NotificationTemplates, and OutboxMessages; the notification center must be fully local-only, supporting in-app message records, template variables, retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff for internal delivery, delivery receipts, reach stats, and per-user frequency caps (default 20 messages/day) with blacklist flags. Security requirements include encrypted-at-rest fields for sensitive attributes (AES-256 for recovery answers), masking in logs, strict password policy (12+ chars, complexity, last 5 reuse blocked), account lock after 10 failed attempts in 15 minutes, and idempotency keys for all mutating operations (stored for 24 hours). Non-functional requirements include p95 latency under 300 ms for typical queries on a single machine, import throughput of 10,000 rows per batch, full auditability of permission changes, and operational observability via structured logs and database-backed job state for import/notification retries..

1	# TripForge — Feature Overview
2	
3	Offline-first backend API platform for travel itinerary planning, data ingestion, and model-assisted recommendations. Built with Express (TypeScript) + Prisma + MySQL. No UI. Pure backend.
4	
5	Implementation priority policy:
6	- P0: Security-critical controls (auth, authz, ownership, isolation, lockout, idempotency)
7	- P1: Core itinerary and resource workflows
8	- P2: Optimization, model decisioning, notifications, and operational polish
9	
10	Frozen clarification baseline (must be followed during implementation):
11	- Organizer scope is owner-only; admin is global.
12	- Refresh tokens are per-device.
13	- Device cap is strict at 5; 6th device login is rejected until removal.
14	- Unusual-city change triggers challenge with rate limit.
15	- Travel matrix is directed (non-symmetric).
16	- Missing business hours/closures means no restriction from that rule.
17	- Rules override model output in combined decisioning.
18	- Request ID is generated server-side when client omits `X-Request-Id`.
19	
20	Source of frozen decisions: `docs/questions.md`.
21	
22	---
23	
24	## Authentication and Identity
25	
26	What it does: Local username/password login with JWT, refresh tokens, device registration, and unusual-location detection.
27	
28	What needs to be built:
29	- Registration endpoint with bcrypt password hashing
30	- Login endpoint with JWT issuance (access token 30 min, refresh token 14 days)
31	- Refresh token endpoint
32	- Logout (revoke refresh token)
33	- Password change endpoint (enforces last-5-reuse policy)
34	- Password recovery via security questions (no email/SMS)
35	- Device registration (max 5 active devices per user)
36	- Unusual-location detection based on last-known city string from client
37	- Rate-limited challenge prompts on unusual location
38	- Account lockout after 10 failed attempts in 15 minutes
39	- Audit log on login, logout, password change, device registration
40	
41	---
42	
43	## RBAC and Authorization
44	
45	What it does: Role-based access control with permission points and menu groupings.
46	
47	What needs to be built:
48	- Role CRUD (Admin only)
49	- Permission point CRUD (Admin only)
50	- Menu CRUD — logical capability bundles grouping permission points
51	- Role-to-permission-point assignment
52	- User-to-role assignment
53	- API-level authorization middleware enforcing permission points
54	- Data-scope rules: Organizer sees only own itineraries; Admin sees all
55	- Full auditability of all permission changes
56	
57	---
58	
59	## Itinerary Management
60	
61	What it does: Create and manage travel itineraries with day-based time slots and conflict validation.
62	
63	What needs to be built:
64	- Itinerary CRUD (title, destination, start/end dates)
65	- Add/update/remove items into day-based time slots (day_number + start_time + end_time)
66	- Conflict validation:
67	  - Overlap detection between items on the same day
68	  - Minimum 15-minute buffer between items
69	  - Business hours enforcement per resource
70	  - Closure date enforcement per resource
71	  - Minimum dwell time enforcement per resource
72	  - Travel time matrix enforcement between consecutive items
73	- Versioned revision records on every save (with diff metadata)
74	- Share token generation (valid 7 days, same deployment only)
75	- Itinerary export (standardized package format)
76	- Version history endpoint
77	
78	---
79	
80	## Route Planning and Optimization
81	
82	What it does: Deterministic heuristic-based route optimization with explainable suggestions.
83	
84	What needs to be built:
85	- Same-area clustering (group items by city/region)
86	- Shortest-path approximation (nearest-neighbor algorithm)
87	- Ranked suggestion list (top 3 arrangements per day)
88	- Explainability payload per suggestion (reason string, estimated time saved)
89	- Travel time matrix CRUD (per city/region, per transport mode)
90	
91	---
92	
93	## Resource Management
94	
95	What it does: Manage attractions, lodging, meals, and meetings with business hours and closures.
96	
97	What needs to be built:
98	- Resource CRUD (name, type, location, min dwell time)
99	- Business hours CRUD per resource (day-of-week, open/close time)
100	- Closure dates CRUD per resource (date + reason)
101	- Travel time matrix entries between resources
102	
103	---
104	
105	## Data Import/Export
106	
107	What it does: Bulk import via Excel/CSV with pre-validation, deduplication, and rollback.
108	
109	What needs to be built:
110	- Excel/CSV template download per entity type
111	- Bulk upload endpoint (multipart form)
112	- Pre-validation pass: schema, required fields, type checks — returns row-level error annotations before commit
113	- Deduplication by configurable key (default: resource name + street_line + city)
114	- Commit endpoint to apply validated rows
115	- Rollback endpoint (available within 10-minute window per batch)
116	- Import batch status and error report endpoints
117	- Throughput target: 10,000 rows per batch
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Model Management and Inference
122	
123	What it does: Register, version, and run ML models locally with explainable inference.
124	
125	What needs to be built:
126	- Model registry CRUD (name, semantic version, type: PMML/ONNX/custom)
127	- Activation status management (inactive, active, canary)
128	- A/B allocation configuration (group name + percentage)
129	- Canary rollout management
130	- Inference endpoint: execute model locally via adapter
131	- Combined rule-and-model decisioning
132	- Explainability payload: top contributing features, confidence bands, applied rules
133	
134	---
135	
136	## Notification Center
137	
138	What it does: Fully local in-app notifications with templates, retry, and frequency caps.
139	
140	What needs to be built:
141	- Notification template CRUD (code, subject, body with {{variable}} placeholders)
142	- Send notification to user (resolves template variables)
143	- In-app notification list per user (with read/unread filter)
144	- Mark notification as read
145	- Delivery receipts and reach stats
146	- Retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff for failed deliveries
147	- Per-user frequency cap (default 20 messages/day)
148	- User blacklist flag
149	- Outbox pattern for reliable delivery
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Security
154	
155	What it does: Encryption, masking, strict password policy, immutable audit trail.
156	
157	What needs to be built:
158	- bcrypt password hashing (rounds=12)
159	- AES-256-GCM field-level encryption for security question answers and sensitive fields
160	- Strict password policy: 12+ chars, complexity (upper, lower, digit, special), last 5 reuse blocked
161	- Masking of sensitive fields in audit log exports
162	- Append-only audit_logs (no DELETE for app DB role)
163	- Idempotency keys for all mutating operations (stored 24 hours)
164	- Request IDs on every request (X-Request-Id header)
165	- Structured logging with sensitive field masking
166	
167	---
168	
169	## Observability
170	
171	What it does: Structured logs and database-backed job state for operational visibility.
172	
173	What needs to be built:
174	- Winston structured JSON logging on every request/response
175	- Database-backed job state for import batches and notification retries
176	- Import batch status endpoint
177	- Notification delivery stats endpoint
178

156	- Per-user frequency cap (default 20/day), blacklist flag
157	- Delivery receipts and reach stats endpoint
158	- Background job: outbox processor (every 30s)
159	- Unit tests: template variable resolution, retry backoff, frequency cap
160	- Integration tests: send, read, retry, cap enforcement
161	
162	---
163	
164	## Slice 11 — Security Hardening and Audit Log
165	Done when:
166	- AES-256-GCM encryption verified on all sensitive fields
167	- Strict password policy enforced (12+ chars, complexity, last-5 reuse)
168	- Masking in audit log exports (password_hash, encrypted fields → [REDACTED])
169	- Append-only audit_logs (no DELETE for app DB role)
170	- Idempotency keys on all mutating operations (stored 24h)
171	- Audit log query endpoint (Admin only)
172	- Audit log CSV export with masking
173	- Integration tests: audit log read, masking in export, 403 for non-admin
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Slice 12 — Final Polish
178	Done when:
179	- `run_tests.sh` passes all unit + integration tests
180	- `docker compose up` cold start works
181	- README has startup command, service addresses, test credentials
182	- No node_modules, dist, or compiled output in repo
183	- No real credentials in any config file
184	- Swagger UI available at `/api/docs`
185	- All p95 read queries have proper indexes
186	- Import throughput verified at 10,000 rows
187	
188	---
189	
190	## Slice 13 — Delivery Acceptance Gate (Final Test)
191	
192	Done when:
193	- Item-by-item acceptance review is written with evidence (`path:line`) for every major criterion (runnable, completeness, architecture, engineering quality, prompt fitness, test coverage)
194	- Security-first review is explicit for authentication, route-level authorization, object-level authorization, and user data isolation
195	- Static test coverage audit maps prompt requirement points to unit/integration test cases and marks each as sufficient/basic/insufficient/missing
196	- Environment restriction notes are separated from product defects (e.g., sandbox/docker permission limits)
197	- Findings are prioritized by severity: Blocking, High, Medium, Low
198	- Report includes reproducible verification commands and expected results for each section
199	- Final acceptance report is stored under `./.tmp/*.md`
200

1	# TripForge — Design Document
2	
3	## 1. Overview
4	
5	TripForge is an offline-first backend API platform for travel itinerary planning, data ingestion, and model-assisted recommendations. No UI. Pure REST API built with Express (TypeScript) + Prisma + MySQL. Runs on a single Docker host with no external connectivity.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 2. Architecture
10	
11	```
12	HTTP Client (Postman / frontend)
13	  │
14	  ▼
15	Express HTTP Server (port 3000)
16	  ├── Global Error Handler          → structured JSON errors
17	  ├── Request ID Middleware         → X-Request-Id on every request
18	  ├── JWT Auth Middleware           → validates Bearer token
19	  ├── RBAC Middleware               → permission-point enforcement
20	  ├── Idempotency Middleware        → deduplication for mutating ops
21	  ├── Audit Middleware              → append-only audit trail
22	  └── Domain Controllers/Services
23	        │
24	        ▼
25	   Prisma ORM layer
26	        │
27	        ▼
28	   MySQL 8 (port 3306)
29	```
30	
31	---
32	
33	## 3. Technology Stack
34	
35	| Layer | Choice |
36	|---|---|
37	| HTTP framework | Express (TypeScript) |
38	| ORM | Prisma |
39	| Database | MySQL 8 |
40	| Auth | JWT (local, no external IdP) |
41	| Password hashing | bcrypt (rounds=12) |
42	| Field encryption | AES-256-GCM |
43	| Validation | zod |
44	| Scheduling | node-cron |
45	| API docs | Swagger UI (swagger-ui-express + openapi spec) |
46	| Logging | Winston with structured JSON |
47	| Excel/CSV | exceljs + csv-parse |
48	| Container | Docker + docker-compose |
49	
50	---
51	
52	## 4. Module Responsibilities
53	
54	| Module | Responsibility |
55	|---|---|
56	| `auth` | Login, JWT, refresh tokens, device registration, unusual-location detection |
57	| `users` | User CRUD, password policy, account lockout, security questions |
58	| `rbac` | Roles, permission points, menu groupings, role bindings |
59	| `itineraries` | Itinerary CRUD, versioning, day-slot management, conflict validation |
60	| `resources` | Attractions, lodging, meals, meetings; business hours, closures |
61	| `routing` | Route optimization heuristics, explainable ranked suggestions |
62	| `import` | Excel/CSV bulk import, pre-validation, row-level errors, rollback |
63	| `models` | ML model registry, semantic versioning, A/B allocations, inference |
64	| `notifications` | Local in-app notifications, templates, retry, frequency caps |
65	| `audit` | Append-only audit log, immutable records |
66	| `common` | Middleware, encryption, error handling, idempotency |
67	
68	---
69

309	### Audit and Idempotency
310	
311	```

330	```
331	
332	---
333	
334	## 6. Key Flows
335	
336	### JWT + Refresh Token Flow
337	
338	```
339	1. POST /auth/login {username, password, deviceFingerprint, lastKnownCity}
340	2. Verify password (bcrypt)
341	3. Check account status (locked/suspended → 401/403)
342	4. Check device count ≤ 5, register device if new
343	5. Detect unusual location: compare lastKnownCity to device.last_known_city
344	6. If unusual: rate-limited challenge prompt (429 with challenge token)
345	7. Issue access token (30 min) + refresh token (14 days)
346	8. Store refresh token hash in DB
347	9. Return tokens
348	
349	POST /auth/refresh {refreshToken}
350	1. Validate refresh token hash in DB
351	2. Check not revoked, not expired
352	3. Issue new access token
353	4. Return new access token
354	```
355	
356	### Itinerary Conflict Validation
357	
358	```
359	1. POST /itineraries/:id/items {resourceId, dayNumber, startTime, endTime}
360	2. Load all existing items for same day
361	3. Check overlap: new item start/end overlaps any existing item → 409
362	4. Check 15-min buffer: gap between adjacent items < 15 min → 409
363	5. Check resource business hours: item outside open hours → 400
364	6. Check resource closures: item on closure date → 400
365	7. Check min dwell time: duration < resource.min_dwell_minutes → 400
366	8. Check travel time: travel from previous item > available gap → 409
367	9. INSERT itinerary_item
368	10. Create new itinerary_version with diff metadata
369	```
370	
371	### Route Optimization
372	
373	```
374	1. GET /itineraries/:id/optimize
375	2. Load all items for each day
376	3. For each day:
377	   a. Cluster items by area (same city/region grouping)
378	   b. Within each cluster, apply nearest-neighbor shortest-path approximation
379	   c. Score each arrangement by total travel time
380	4. Return ranked list (top 3) with explainable reasons per suggestion
381	   - reason: "Groups Museum District items together, saves ~45 min travel"
382	```
383	
384	### Bulk Import Flow
385	
386	```
387	1. POST /import/upload {file, entityType, idempotencyKey}
388	2. Check idempotency key not already used
389	3. Parse file (Excel/CSV)
390	4. Pre-validate all rows (schema, required fields, types)
391	5. Deduplicate by configurable key (default: name + street_line + city)
392	6. Return validation report with row-level errors before committing
393	7. POST /import/:batchId/commit — commit valid rows
394	8. Store batch with rollback_until = now + 10 min
395	9. POST /import/:batchId/rollback — available within 10 min window
396	```
397	
398	### Model Inference
399	
400	```
401	1. POST /models/:id/infer {input, context}
402	2. Load active model (or canary based on A/B allocation)
403	3. Execute inference via adapter (PMML/ONNX/custom process)
404	4. Apply combined rule-and-model decisioning
405	5. Return result + explainability payload:
406	   {
407	     prediction: ...,
408	     confidence: 0.87,
409	     confidenceBand: [0.82, 0.92],
410	     topFeatures: [{feature: "...", contribution: 0.34}, ...],
411	     appliedRules: [{rule: "...", triggered: true}, ...]
412	   }
413	```
414	
415	---
416	
417	## 7. Security Design
418	
419	- Passwords: bcrypt rounds=12, min 12 chars, complexity enforced, last 5 reuse blocked
420	- JWT: HS256, access token 30 min, refresh token 14 days, secret from env
421	- Field encryption: AES-256-GCM for security question answers, sensitive notes
422	- Audit log: INSERT-only for app DB role, no UPDATE/DELETE
423	- Account lockout: 10 failed attempts in 15 min → locked
424	- Device limit: max 5 active devices per user
425	- Unusual location: challenge prompt if city differs from last known
426	- Idempotency keys: stored 24 hours, globally unique per operation type
427	- Sensitive fields masked in audit log exports
428	- Request IDs: UUID per request, attached to all log lines and `X-Request-Id` header
429	
430	---
431	
432	## 8. Background Jobs
433	
434	| Job | Interval | Description |
435	|---|---|---|
436	| Notification outbox processor | 30s | Deliver pending notifications with exponential backoff |
437	| Notification retry | 1 min | Retry failed notifications (max 3 attempts) |
438	| Idempotency key cleanup | 1 hour | Delete expired idempotency keys |
439	| Refresh token cleanup | 1 hour | Delete expired/revoked refresh tokens |
440	| Import rollback expiry | 5 min | Mark import batches past rollback window |
441	| Daily notification cap reset | midnight | Reset daily message counts |
442	
443	---
444	
445	## 9. Error Handling
446	
447	All errors return:
448	```json
449	{
450	  "statusCode": 400,
451	  "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
452	  "message": "human readable message",
453	  "requestId": "uuid"
454	}
455	```
456	
457	Standard codes: VALIDATION_ERROR (400), UNAUTHORIZED (401), FORBIDDEN (403), NOT_FOUND (404), CONFLICT (409), IDEMPOTENCY_CONFLICT (409), RATE_LIMITED (429), INTERNAL_ERROR (500)
458	
459	---
460	
461	## 10. Docker Setup
462	
463	```yaml
464	services:
465	  api:
466	    build: .
467	    ports:
468	      - "3000:3000"
469	    environment:
470	      DATABASE_URL: mysql://tripforge:tripforge@db:3306/tripforge
471	      JWT_SECRET: ${JWT_SECRET}
472	      ENCRYPTION_KEY: ${ENCRYPTION_KEY}
473	    depends_on:
474	      db:
475	        condition: service_healthy
476	
477	  db:
478	    image: mysql:8
479	    environment:
480	      MYSQL_USER: tripforge
481	      MYSQL_PASSWORD: tripforge
482	      MYSQL_DATABASE: tripforge
483	      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: rootpassword
484	    healthcheck:
485	      test: ["CMD", "mysqladmin", "ping", "-h", "localhost"]
486	      interval: 5s
487	      retries: 10
488	    volumes:
489	      - mysqldata:/var/lib/mysql
490	
491	volumes:
492	  mysqldata:
493	```
494	
495	---
496	
497	## 11. Performance Strategy
498	
499	- Index all foreign keys
500	- Index `itinerary_items.itinerary_id`, `itinerary_items.day_number`
501	- Index `audit_logs.created_at`, `audit_logs.actor_id`
502	- Index `idempotency_keys.expires_at` for cleanup job
503	- Index `notifications.user_id`, `notifications.delivered`
504	- Index `import_batches.idempotency_key`
505	- Prisma query optimization for complex joins
506	- Connection pool: min 2, max 10
507	- Import batches processed in chunks of 500 rows
508	
509	---
510	
511	## 12. Implementation Readiness Rules (Must Follow)
512	
513	1. Security-first ordering
514	- Implement authentication, route authorization, object-level authorization, and data isolation before non-critical modules.
515	
516	2. No ambiguous behavior in code
517	- Use `docs/questions.md` as binding decisions for ambiguous prompt areas.
518	- If a new ambiguity appears, add Question + Assumption + Solution before implementing.
519	
520	3. Test-before-expansion rule
521	- For each slice, add or update unit/integration tests in the same change set.
522	- Minimum requirement to close a slice: happy path + one high-risk exception path.
523	
524	4. API-contract consistency
525	- Runtime responses must follow `docs/api-spec.md` status codes and payload shape.
526	- Update API spec and tests together if implementation-level changes are unavoidable.
527	
528	5. Logging and data protection
529	- Never log tokens, raw passwords, or decrypted sensitive fields.
530	- All logs include request ID and category (`auth`, `rbac`, `itinerary`, `import`, `model`, `notification`).
531	
532	6. Acceptance gate requirement
533	- Before marking final completion, pass every gate in `docs/acceptance-checklist.md` and update `docs/AI-self-test.md`.
534

85	- [ ] Unit tests exist for core services (auth, validation, routing, import, model, notification)
86	- [ ] Integration tests exist for major API modules
87	- [ ] Security tests exist for 401, 403, ownership violations, and data isolation
88	- [ ] Exception path tests exist (400, 404, 409, 429, idempotency conflicts)
89	- [ ] Boundary tests exist (pagination, empty datasets, time boundaries, rollback window)
90	- [ ] Sensitive-info leakage tests exist for logs/responses/exports
91	- [ ] README documents exact commands to run all tests
92	
93	Exit condition:
94	- [ ] Tests are sufficient to catch the vast majority of critical defects.
95	
96	---
97	
98	## F) Submission Gate
99	
100	- [ ] `docs/design.md`, `docs/features.md`, `docs/api-spec.md`, `docs/questions.md` remain aligned with implementation
101	- [ ] `docs/AI-self-test.md` updated to completed state where applicable
102	- [ ] `sessions/develop-1.json` exists
103	- [ ] `metadata.json` exists with required fields
104	- [ ] No real credentials committed
105	- [ ] No unnecessary artifacts in final package (`node_modules`, compiled outputs, temp exports/uploads)
106	
107	Exit condition:
108	- [ ] Delivery package is review-ready and reproducible.

1	
2	# Questions and Clarifications — TripForge
3	
4	---
5	
6	## 1. Organizer vs Admin Data Scope — What Roles Exist?
7	
8	**Question:** The prompt mentions "Organizer" and "Admin" as example roles with different data scopes. Are these the only roles, or are there more?
9	
10	**Assumption:** The system ships with two seed roles: `admin` (full access) and `organizer` (own-data access). Additional roles can be created via the RBAC API. The data-scope rule is enforced at the service layer: Organizer queries are always filtered by `owner_id = currentUser.id`.
11	
12	**Solution:** RBAC middleware checks permission points. A separate data-scope check in the itinerary service filters by owner_id for non-admin users.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 2. Device Registration — What Is a Device Fingerprint?
17	
18	**Question:** The prompt says devices are registered with a fingerprint hash. Who generates this fingerprint — the client or the server?
19	
20	**Assumption:** The client generates and sends a device fingerprint string (e.g., hash of browser/app metadata). The server stores a bcrypt/SHA-256 hash of it. The server does not generate fingerprints.
21	
22	**Solution:** `POST /auth/login` accepts optional `deviceFingerprint` string. Server hashes it before storing. If not provided, a server-generated UUID is used as the device identifier.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## 3. Unusual Location Detection — What Triggers a Challenge?
27	
28	**Question:** The prompt says unusual-location detection is based on "last-known city string supplied by the client." What exactly triggers a challenge — any city change, or only a significant one?
29	
30	**Assumption:** Any change in city string compared to the device's stored `last_known_city` triggers a challenge prompt. The challenge is a rate-limited 429 response with a `challengeToken`. The client must re-submit with the challenge token to proceed.
31	
32	**Solution:** On login, compare `lastKnownCity` to `device.last_known_city`. If different and device is known, return 429 with a short-lived challenge token (5 min TTL). Client re-submits with `challengeToken` to confirm the new location.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## 4. Refresh Token — Per Device or Per User?
37	
38	**Question:** The prompt says refresh tokens have a 14-day TTL. Are they scoped per device or per user?
39	
40	**Assumption:** Refresh tokens are scoped per device. Each device gets its own refresh token. Revoking a device also revokes its refresh token. A user can have up to 5 active refresh tokens (one per device).
41	
42	**Solution:** `refresh_tokens` table has `device_id` FK. Deleting a device cascades to its refresh token.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 5. Itinerary Conflict — 15-Minute Buffer — Is It Configurable?
47	
48	**Question:** The prompt says "default minimum buffer of 15 minutes between items." Does "default" imply it can be changed per itinerary or per user?
49	
50	**Assumption:** The 15-minute buffer is a system-wide default enforced on all itineraries. It is not configurable per itinerary or user in this version. The word "default" in the prompt is treated as a fixed constant.
51	
52	**Solution:** Buffer constant defined in config (e.g., `MIN_BUFFER_MINUTES=15`). Validation service uses this constant.
53	
54	---
55	
56	## 6. Travel Time Matrix — Is It Bidirectional?
57	
58	**Question:** The travel time matrix stores from/to pairs. Is travel time assumed to be symmetric (A→B = B→A), or must both directions be stored?
59	
60	**Assumption:** Travel times are not assumed symmetric (e.g., uphill vs downhill). Both directions must be stored explicitly. If a direction is missing, the validation service skips the travel time check for that pair (does not block scheduling).
61	
62	**Solution:** `travel_time_matrices` stores directed pairs. Validation service looks up `(from, to)` specifically. Missing entry = no travel time constraint applied.
63	
64	---
65	
66	## 7. Itinerary Versioning — What Triggers a New Version?
67	
68	**Question:** The prompt says "every save creates a versioned revision record." Does this mean every PATCH to the itinerary, or only when items are added/removed/moved?
69	
70	**Assumption:** A new version is created on every mutation that changes the itinerary's content: adding/removing/updating items, and updating itinerary metadata (title, dates). Simple status changes (draft → published) do not create a new version.
71	
72	**Solution:** Version creation is triggered in the service layer after any content-changing operation. Diff metadata compares the new snapshot to the previous version's snapshot.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## 8. Share Token — Can It Be Revoked?
77	
78	**Question:** The prompt says share tokens are valid for 7 days. Can the owner revoke a share token before it expires?
79	
80	**Assumption:** Yes — calling `POST /itineraries/:id/share` again generates a new token and invalidates the previous one. There is no explicit revoke endpoint; re-generating is the revocation mechanism.
81	
82	**Solution:** `itineraries.share_token` is overwritten on each call to the share endpoint. Old tokens become invalid immediately.
83	
84	---
85	
86	## 9. Import Deduplication — What Happens to Duplicates?
87	
88	**Question:** The prompt says deduplication uses a configurable key. When a duplicate is detected, is the row skipped, updated, or flagged as an error?
89	
90	**Assumption:** Duplicates are skipped (not updated, not errored). The validation report marks them as `skipped_duplicate` with the matching existing record ID. The user can override this behavior by passing `deduplicationStrategy: upsert` to update existing records.
91	
92	**Solution:** Import service checks for existing records by dedup key before insert. Default behavior: skip. Optional `deduplicationStrategy` param supports `skip` (default) or `upsert`.
93	
94	---
95	
96	## 10. Model Inference — How Are PMML/ONNX Models Executed?
97	
98	**Question:** The prompt says inference runs via "Java-side adapters (PMML/ONNX or custom process)." Does this mean a Java subprocess is spawned, or is there a Java service running alongside?
99	
100	**Assumption:** For this implementation, PMML/ONNX inference is handled by spawning a child process (Node.js `child_process.spawn`) that calls a bundled Java JAR or Python script. The adapter pattern abstracts the execution method. For the submission, a mock adapter is provided that returns deterministic fake results, with the real adapter interface documented.
101	
102	**Solution:** `ModelAdapter` interface with `infer(input, config): Promise<InferenceResult>`. Implementations: `PmmlAdapter`, `OnnxAdapter`, `CustomAdapter`. Each spawns the appropriate process. A `MockAdapter` is used in tests.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## 11. Notification Frequency Cap — Per Day or Rolling Window?
107	
108	**Question:** The prompt says "per-user frequency caps (default 20 messages/day)." Is this a calendar day (midnight reset) or a rolling 24-hour window?
109	
110	**Assumption:** Calendar day reset at midnight UTC. A `daily_message_count` counter is reset by a scheduled job at midnight. This is simpler and more predictable than a rolling window.
111	
112	**Solution:** `user_notification_settings` stores `daily_cap` (default 20). A cron job resets a `daily_sent_count` counter at midnight UTC. Notification service checks count before sending.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## 12. Password Recovery — How Many Security Questions?
117	
118	**Question:** The prompt says password recovery uses security questions but doesn't specify how many are required.
119	
120	**Assumption:** Users must set exactly 2 security questions at registration. Recovery requires correct answers to both. Questions are free-text (user writes their own question and answer).
121	
122	**Solution:** Registration validates that exactly 2 security questions are provided. Recovery endpoint requires both answers to match (AES-256 decrypt + compare).
123	
124	---
125	
126	## 13. Idempotency Keys — Which Operations Require Them?
127	
128	**Question:** The prompt says "idempotency keys for all mutating operations." Does this mean every POST/PATCH/DELETE, or only specific high-risk operations?
129	
130	**Assumption:** Idempotency keys are required on: order-like operations (import upload/commit), model inference calls, and any operation explicitly marked in the API spec. Standard CRUD (create resource, update itinerary) does not require idempotency keys unless the client provides one voluntarily.
131	
132	**Solution:** Idempotency middleware checks for `Idempotency-Key` header on POST requests. If present, stores and deduplicates. Required on: `POST /import/upload`, `POST /import/:id/commit`. Optional on all other POSTs.
133	
134	---
135	
136	## 14. A/B Allocation — How Is the Group Determined?
137	
138	**Question:** The prompt mentions A/B allocations and canary rollouts for models. How is a user assigned to a group?
139	
140	**Assumption:** Group assignment is deterministic based on a hash of `userId + modelName`. This ensures the same user always gets the same model version, avoiding inconsistent experiences. Percentage thresholds determine group boundaries.
141	
142	**Solution:** `hash(userId + modelName) % 100` determines the user's bucket. If bucket < canary percentage, use canary model; otherwise use active model.
143	
144	---
145	
146	## 15. Audit Log — Which Actions Are Logged?
147	
148	**Question:** The prompt says "full auditability of permission changes" and "immutable" audit logs. What is the full list of audited actions?
149	
150	**Assumption:** Audited actions include: login, logout, password change, device registration/removal, user create/update/delete, role create/update/delete, permission assignment, itinerary create/update/delete, import commit/rollback, model activation, notification send.
151	
152	**Solution:** Audit middleware intercepts responses and logs action + resource_type + resource_id + actor_id + IP. Sensitive fields (password_hash, encrypted answers) are masked as `[REDACTED]` in the detail JSON.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## 16. Device Cap — What Happens at 5 Active Devices?
157	
158	**Question:** The prompt caps devices at 5 active per user. What happens when a 6th login occurs?
159	
160	**Assumption:** The login is rejected with a 409 error until the user removes an existing device via `DELETE /auth/devices/{id}`. No automatic eviction.
161	
162	**Solution:** On login, if a new device would exceed the cap, return `409 DEVICE_LIMIT_REACHED` and include the current device list so the client can prompt removal.
163	
164	---
165	
166	## 17. Unusual-Location Challenge — What Rate Limit Applies?
167	
168	**Question:** The prompt requires rate-limited challenge prompts for unusual locations, but does not define the limit or window.
169	
170	**Assumption:** Allow up to 3 challenge prompts per user per hour. Further attempts return `429` with a `retryAfterSeconds` value.
171	
172	**Solution:** Store challenge attempts in a rolling 1-hour window keyed by user+device. Enforce the cap before issuing a new challenge token.
173	
174	---
175	
176	## 18. Missing Business Hours/Closures — How Should Validation Behave?
177	
178	**Question:** If a resource lacks business hours or closure dates, should scheduling be blocked or allowed?
179	
180	**Assumption:** Missing hours/closures mean no restriction is applied. Scheduling is allowed as long as overlap/buffer/dwell rules pass.
181	
182	**Solution:** Validation checks business hours and closures only when data exists; otherwise it skips those constraints.
183	
184	---
185	
186	## 19. Itinerary Export — What Is the Standard Package Format?
187	
188	**Question:** The prompt requires exporting a standardized itinerary package but does not specify format.
189	
190	**Assumption:** Export is a JSON payload with a `schemaVersion`, itinerary metadata, day items, and linked resources; no zip container.
191	
192	**Solution:** `GET /itineraries/{id}/export` returns `application/json` with the standardized schema and a `schemaVersion` field for forward compatibility.
193	
194	---
195	
196	## 20. Model Artifacts — How Are Files Stored or Loaded?
197	
198	**Question:** Model registration is defined, but there is no upload endpoint. Where do PMML/ONNX artifacts live?
199	
200	**Assumption:** Model files are placed manually in the local `models/` directory and referenced by a `filePath` in the model `config`. No network downloads.
201	
202	**Solution:** The model registry stores metadata + `config.filePath`. Adapters load files from disk only.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## 21. Rule-and-Model Decisioning — Which Takes Precedence?
207	
208	**Question:** The prompt requires combined rule-and-model decisioning. If rules and model disagree, which wins?
209	
210	**Assumption:** Rules are hard constraints and override the model. The model provides a suggestion only when rules pass.
211	
212	**Solution:** Apply rules first; if any rule blocks, return a rule-based decision with `appliedRules`. Otherwise return the model prediction with the rule summary included.
213	
214	---
215	
216	## 22. Request IDs — What If the Client Omits X-Request-Id?
217	
218	**Question:** The prompt mandates request IDs on every request. Should the server generate one when absent?
219	
220	**Assumption:** Yes. The server generates a UUID if `X-Request-Id` is missing and echoes it back in responses and logs.
221	
222	**Solution:** Request-id middleware sets `req.id` from header or generates one, then adds `X-Request-Id` to the response.
223

---

## What I've Learned — TripForge Project

**Project:** TripForge — offline-first backend API for travel itinerary planning, data ingestion, and ML-assisted recommendations. Pure backend, no UI. Express (TypeScript) + Prisma + MySQL, single Docker container.

**Current State:** Documentation/planning phase only. The `repo/` directory has only `.gitkeep` placeholder files — zero implementation code, no `package.json`, no Prisma schema, no tests, no Dockerfile.

**Docs quality is strong** — 9 well-structured docs covering design, API spec (OpenAPI 3.0), features, build order (13 slices), acceptance checklist, 22 clarification Q&As, self-test checklist, folder structure, and a delivery acceptance review.

---

## Gaps & Improvements I Found in the Docs

Here are things that are **missing, inconsistent, or could be improved** across the documentation:

### 1. **API Spec: Missing `POST /notifications/send` endpoint**
The features doc (`features.md:142`) says "Send notification to user (resolves template variables)" but `api-spec.md` has **no endpoint for sending a notification**. There's only list, mark-read, stats, and template CRUD. A `POST /notifications` or `POST /notifications/send` endpoint is needed.

### 2. **API Spec: Missing Role update/delete endpoints**
`features.md:46` says "Role CRUD (Admin only)" but `api-spec.md` only has `GET/POST /roles` and `POST /roles/:id/permissions`. There's no `PATCH /roles/:id` or `DELETE /roles/:id`. Same for permission points — no update/delete endpoints.

### 3. **API Spec: Missing Menu update/delete endpoints**
`features.md:50` says "Menu CRUD" but api-spec only has `GET/POST /menus`. No `PATCH /menus/:id` or `DELETE /menus/:id`.

### 4. **API Spec: Missing `GET /models/:id` details for A/B allocations**
There's `POST /models/:id/ab-allocations` but no `GET` to list existing allocations for a model.

### 5. **Data Model: Missing `password_history` table**
The design says "last 5 reuse blocked" (`design.md:419`) but the data model has no table to store previous password hashes. You need a `password_history` table (user_id, password_hash, created_at) to enforce reuse prevention.

### 6. **Data Model: Missing `daily_sent_count` field**
`questions.md:112` says a `daily_sent_count` counter is reset at midnight, but `user_notification_settings` in `design.md:303` only has `blacklisted`, `daily_cap`, and `updated_at` — no `daily_sent_count` column.

### 7. **Data Model: Missing challenge token storage**
`questions.md:32` describes a short-lived challenge token (5 min TTL) for unusual-location detection, but there's no table or column to store challenge tokens. Needs a `challenge_tokens` table or a column on `devices`.

### 8. **Data Model: Missing `import_batch_rows` or staging table**
The import flow does pre-validation then commit, but there's no table to store the validated rows between upload and commit. Without this, the system can't implement the two-phase upload→commit flow.

### 9. **Questions.md Q13: Contradicts the prompt**
The prompt explicitly says "idempotency keys for **all** mutating operations." Q13's assumption narrows this to only imports and optional elsewhere. This contradicts the prompt requirement. The implementation should enforce idempotency keys on all POST/PATCH/DELETE or at minimum make the middleware always active.

### 10. **API Spec: `POST /travel-times` should be upsert**
The summary says "Create or update travel time entry" but there's no documented behavior for how the upsert key works (presumably `fromResourceId + toResourceId + transportMode`). Should be explicit.

### 11. **Data Model: Missing unique constraint on `travel_time_matrices`**
The combination of `(from_resource_id, to_resource_id, transport_mode)` should be UNIQUE to support upsert behavior, but no such constraint is documented.

### 12. **Design: Background job for share token expiry cleanup is missing**
Share tokens expire after 7 days, but there's no cleanup job listed in the background jobs table (`design.md:434-441`). Expired tokens should be cleaned up periodically.

### 13. **API Spec: `GET /auth/me` response schema is empty**
The endpoint exists but has no response schema defined — should document the user profile fields returned.

### 14. **Data Model: `itinerary_items` missing `updated_at`**
Items can be updated via `PATCH`, but there's no `updated_at` timestamp column.

### 15. **Build Order: Slice 11 (Security Hardening) timing concern**
Idempotency keys and audit logging are deferred to Slice 11, but the build order says "Security-first ordering" in `design.md:513`. Idempotency middleware should be in Slice 1 (Foundation) or Slice 2, not Slice 11, since it's needed for all mutating operations from the start.

---

# TripForge
## Quick Start
cp .env.example .env
docker compose up --build
## Ports: API http://localhost:3000 | Swagger http://localhost:3000/api/docs
## Test Credentials: username: admin | password: Admin123!
## Run Tests (inside container): docker compose exec api sh run_tests.sh

---

## STEP 2 — Prisma schema (Slice 1 minimal)

prisma/schema.prisma:
- datasource db: provider mysql, url env DATABASE_URL
- generator client: provider prisma-client-js, output ../src/models/prisma
- Model AuditLog: id String @id @default(uuid()), action String, detail Json?, traceId String?, createdAt DateTime @default(now())

prisma/seed.ts: console.log("Seed complete — no data for Slice 1")

---

## STEP 3 — Slice 1 source files

src/config/environment.ts — typed config from process.env: port, databaseUrl, jwtSecret, encryptionKey, accessTokenTtl, refreshTokenTtl

src/config/database.ts — export PrismaClient singleton

src/config/auth.ts — export JWT constants: accessTokenTtl, refreshTokenTtl, algorithm HS256

src/utils/logger.ts — Winston JSON logger, every line includes traceId from AsyncLocalStorage

src/utils/errors.ts — AppError class with statusCode + code. Constants: VALIDATION_ERROR, UNAUTHORIZED, FORBIDDEN, NOT_FOUND, CONFLICT, INTERNAL_ERROR

src/middleware/audit.middleware.ts — generates UUID traceId per request, stores in AsyncLocalStorage, sets X-Trace-Id response header, logs method+path+status on response finish

src/middleware/auth.middleware.ts — stub passthrough only (implemented in Slice 2)

src/app.ts:
- helmet, compression
- audit middleware globally
- GET /health → { status: "ok", timestamp }
- /api/docs → swagger-ui-express with minimal stub spec
- global error handler → { statusCode, code, message, traceId }

src/server.ts:
- connect Prisma, app.listen on PORT, log "Server started on port X"

---

## STEP 4 — Slice 1 tests

unit_tests/bootstrap.spec.ts:
- Test 1: environment config loads without throwing
- Test 2: AppError sets statusCode and code correctly

Offline-first backend API platform for travel itinerary planning, data ingestion, and model-assisted recommendations.

## Quick Start

```bash
cp .env.example .env

```

|---------|-----|

| Swagger | http://localhost:3000/api/docs |

## Test Credentials

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| username | admin |
| password | Admin123!Admin |

## Run Tests

Inside the container:

```bash
docker compose exec api sh run_tests.sh
```

Or run individually:

```bash
docker compose exec api npm run test:unit
docker compose exec api npm run test:api
```

model User {
  id                String             @id @default(uuid())
  username          String             @unique @db.VarChar(255)
  passwordHash      String             @db.VarChar(255) @map("password_hash")
  role              String             @default("organizer") @db.VarChar(50)
  status            String             @default("active") @db.VarChar(20)
  failedAttempts    Int                @default(0) @map("failed_attempts")
  lockedUntil       DateTime?          @map("locked_until")
  createdAt         DateTime           @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt         DateTime           @updatedAt @map("updated_at")
  devices           Device[]
  refreshTokens     RefreshToken[]
  securityQuestions SecurityQuestion[]
  passwordHistory   PasswordHistory[]
  @@map("users")
}

model SecurityQuestion {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId          String   @map("user_id")
  question        String   @db.Text
  answerEncrypted String   @db.Text @map("answer_encrypted")
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  user            User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  @@map("security_questions")
}

model Device {
  id                String    @id @default(uuid())
  userId            String    @map("user_id")
  fingerprintHash   String    @db.VarChar(255) @map("fingerprint_hash")
  lastSeenAt        DateTime  @map("last_seen_at")
  lastKnownCity     String?   @db.VarChar(255) @map("last_known_city")
  createdAt         DateTime  @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  user              User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  refreshTokens     RefreshToken[]
  @@unique([userId, fingerprintHash])
  @@map("devices")
}

model RefreshToken {
  id        String    @id @default(uuid())
  userId    String    @map("user_id")
  deviceId  String    @map("device_id")
  tokenHash String    @db.VarChar(255) @map("token_hash")
  expiresAt DateTime  @map("expires_at")
  revokedAt DateTime? @map("revoked_at")
  createdAt DateTime  @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  user      User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  device    Device    @relation(fields: [deviceId], references: [id])
  @@map("refresh_tokens")
}

model PasswordHistory {
  id           String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId       String   @map("user_id")
  passwordHash String   @db.VarChar(255) @map("password_hash")
  createdAt    DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  user         User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  @@map("password_history")
}

model IdempotencyKey {
  key           String   @id @db.VarChar(255)
  operationType String   @db.VarChar(100) @map("operation_type")
  responseBody  Json     @map("response_body")
  createdAt     DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  expiresAt     DateTime @map("expires_at")
  @@index([expiresAt])
  @@map("idempotency_keys")
}

STEP 2 — src/types/auth.types.ts
export type UserRole = 'admin' | 'organizer';
export interface JwtPayload { userId: string; username: string; role: UserRole; iat?: number; exp?: number; }
export interface TokenPair { accessToken: string; refreshToken: string; }
export interface DeviceInfo { fingerprint?: string; lastKnownCity?: string; }

STEP 3 — src/utils/crypto.ts
Implement AES-256-GCM encrypt(plaintext: string): string and decrypt(ciphertext: string): string using ENCRYPTION_KEY from env. Format: base64(iv:authTag:ciphertext).

STEP 4 — src/services/auth.service.ts
Implement all methods. Key rules:
- register: min 12 chars, must have uppercase + lowercase + digit + special char, bcrypt rounds=12, store 2 security questions with AES-256 encrypted answers, save password to password_history
- login: verify bcrypt, increment failed_attempts on failure, lock after 10 failures in 15 min (set locked_until = now+15min), on success reset failed_attempts, register device (max 5 active per user — reject with 400 if exceeded), detect unusual location (lastKnownCity differs from device.last_known_city → return 429 with challengeToken), issue accessToken (ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL seconds, HS256), issue refreshToken (REFRESH_TOKEN_TTL seconds), store SHA-256 hash of refreshToken in DB
- refresh: find token by hash, check not revoked and not expired, issue new accessToken
- logout: set revokedAt on refresh token
- changePassword: verify current password, enforce policy, check last 5 password hashes (reject if any bcrypt.compare matches), update hash, add to password_history
- recoverPassword: decrypt stored answers, compare with provided answers (case-insensitive trim), reset password if both match

STEP 5 — src/middleware/auth.middleware.ts
Replace stub with real implementation:
- Extract Bearer token from Authorization header
- Verify with JWT_SECRET using HS256
- Load user from DB, check status === 'active'
- Attach to req.user
- Return 401 if missing/invalid token, 403 if suspended/deleted

STEP 6 — src/controllers/auth.controller.ts
POST /auth/register — { username, password, securityQuestions: [{question, answer}] }
POST /auth/login — { username, password, deviceFingerprint?, lastKnownCity? }
POST /auth/refresh — { refreshToken }
POST /auth/logout — { refreshToken }
PATCH /auth/change-password — { currentPassword, newPassword } (requires auth)
POST /auth/recover — { username, answers: [{question, answer}], newPassword }
GET /auth/me — (requires auth)
GET /auth/devices — (requires auth)
DELETE /auth/devices/:id — (requires auth)

STEP 7 — src/routes/auth.routes.ts + mount in src/app.ts at /auth

STEP 8 — unit_tests/auth.spec.ts
Test these in isolation (mock getPrisma):
- password policy: rejects < 12 chars
- password policy: rejects missing uppercase
- password policy: rejects missing digit
- password policy: rejects missing special char
- password policy: accepts valid password
- bcrypt hash and verify round-trip
- AES-256 encrypt/decrypt round-trip
- JWT sign and verify with correct secret
- JWT verify fails with wrong secret
- lockout: 10 failures sets lockedUntil
- last-5 reuse: rejects password matching any of last 5 hashes

STEP 9 — API_tests/auth.api.spec.ts
All tests run against the live Docker app (supertest against imported app, DB must be running):
- POST /auth/register 201 — returns user id and username
- POST /auth/register 409 — duplicate username
- POST /auth/register 400 — password too short
- POST /auth/register 400 — password missing digit
- POST /auth/login 200 — returns accessToken and refreshToken
- POST /auth/login 401 — wrong password
- POST /auth/login 423 — after 10 failed attempts (locked)
- POST /auth/refresh 200 — returns new accessToken
- POST /auth/refresh 401 — invalid refresh token
- POST /auth/logout 204
- GET /auth/me 200 — with valid Bearer token
- GET /auth/me 401 — no token
- GET /auth/me 401 — expired token
- PATCH /auth/change-password 200
- PATCH /auth/change-password 400 — reuse violation

Slice 2 exit conditions — verify ALL before moving to Slice 3:
- [ ] docker compose up --build starts cleanly
- [ ] Prisma migration for auth models runs without error
- [ ] All Slice 1 tests still pass
- [ ] npm run test:unit passes (unit_tests/auth.spec.ts)
- [ ] npm run test:api passes (API_tests/auth.api.spec.ts)
- [ ] POST /auth/register → POST /auth/login → GET /auth/me full flow works end-to-end

I'll work through the fixes first, then implement Slice 2 systematically.

  expiresAt DateTime  @map("expires_at")
  revokedAt DateTime? @map("revoked_at")
  createdAt DateTime  @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  user      User      @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
  device    Device    @relation(fields: [deviceId], references: [id])

  @@map("refresh_tokens")
}

model PasswordHistory {

  passwordHash String   @db.VarChar(255) @map("password_hash")

The issue is the DB connection. The test is using `DATABASE_URL` to `127.0.0.1:3306` but the default in environment.ts uses `db:3306`. Let me check:
mysql://tripforge:tripforge@127.0.0.1:3306/tripforge

The env var is set. The issue is that Prisma reads `DATABASE_URL` directly from env (via `schema.prisma`), not from our environment.ts. But the test imports `app` which imports `database.ts` which creates a `PrismaClient()` — and that reads the schema's datasource URL. The problem might be that the first test (register) succeeds but then the DB connection dies during the lockout test's rapid-fire requests.

here are three things blocking this project from working correctly in Docker. First, run_tests.sh runs tests directly on the host machine — it should instead check if the containers are running, wait until the API health endpoint responds ok, then run both unit and API tests inside the container. Second, the Dockerfile multi-stage build doesn't copy the Prisma migration files into the runner image, so when the container starts and tries to run npx prisma migrate deploy it will fail because the migration files aren't there — fix the runner stage to include the prisma/ folder. Third, 
database.ts
 exports an uninitialized prisma variable at the bottom of the file — remove that bare export, only getPrisma() should be exported. Fix these three things, then verify by running docker compose up --build and confirming the API starts clean and /health returns ok, then run 
run_tests.sh
 from the project root and confirm all tests pass.

{"file_path": "/home/someone/Documents/eagle-point/108/repo/Dockerfile", "content": "# Stage 1 — Builder
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app

COPY package*.json tsconfig.json ./
COPY prisma/ ./prisma/

RUN npm ci

RUN npx prisma generate

COPY src/ ./src/

RUN npm run build

# Stage 2 — Runner
FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /app

COPY package*.json tsconfig.json jest.config.js ./

RUN npm ci

COPY --from=builder /app/dist/ ./dist/
COPY --from=builder /app/prisma/ ./prisma/

Wait — this won't work because `npm ci` in the builder downloads the Prisma engine binaries for the host platform, but the generated client at `src/models/prisma/` was generated on the host (Linux x86_64) which should match Alpine. But `npm ci` won't run `prisma generate` automatically. The issue is the generated client references engine binaries that come from `@prisma/client` in `node_modules`. Let me check what's in `src/models/prisma/`:

default.d.ts
default.js
edge.d.ts
edge.js
index-browser.js
index.d.ts
index.js
libquery_engine-debian-openssl-3.0.x.so.node
package.json
runtime
schema.prisma
wasm.d.ts
wasm.js

The generated client includes a `libquery_engine-debian-openssl-3.0.x.so.node` binary — that's for Debian, not Alpine (musl). In the builder stage (Alpine), the `tsc` build only compiles TS to JS and doesn't need the engine. But at runtime in the runner (Alpine), it needs the musl engine binary. The solution: run `npx prisma generate` in the runner stage after npm ci, since npm ci will download packages and the generate will create the correct binary for Alpine.

Wait — `prisma generate` needs network access to download engine binaries, which is the root problem. The `|| true` will just silently fail. Let me think about this differently.

The real issue: Docker build can't reach `binaries.prisma.sh`. This is a network restriction in the build environment. The solution is to pre-download the correct engine binary. Let me check if we can set `PRISMA_ENGINES_MIRROR` or use `binaryTargets` in the schema.

Actually, the best approach: add `binaryTargets` to the Prisma schema so `prisma generate` on the host pre-downloads the Alpine engine, then copy it directly.

Clean output — no warnings, all 38 tests pass. Here's a summary of the three fixes:

**FIX 1 — run_tests.sh**: Now checks if containers are running, waits for `/health` to respond OK, then runs both `test:unit` and `test:api` inside the container via `docker compose exec -T`.

**FIX 2 — Dockerfile**: Runner stage now copies the entire `prisma/` directory (including migrations) from the builder via `COPY --from=builder /app/prisma/ ./prisma/`. Also added `binaryTargets = ["native", "linux-musl-openssl-3.0.x"]` to the Prisma schema so the musl engine binary is pre-generated, and a symlink at `dist/models/prisma` so the compiled JS can resolve the Prisma client.

**FIX 3 — database.ts**: Was already clean from the earlier fix session — only exports `getPrisma()`, no bare `prisma` export.

Rewrite the Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml, and run_tests.sh for this project to be clean and professional. Here is exactly what each should look like and why:

For the Dockerfile — simplify it to a single stage like this: start from node:20-alpine, install openssl, set workdir to /app, copy package files, run npm ci, copy everything else including src, prisma, unit_tests, API_tests, jest.config.js, tsconfig.json, then run npm run build, expose 3000, and set the CMD to node dist/server.js. Single stage means everything is in the image including tests, no confusion about what got copied from where.

For docker-compose.yml — change build back to just build: . and remove the network: host from the build block. Add a healthcheck on the api service itself that uses wget to check http://localhost:3000/health every 10 seconds with a 15 second start period and 5 retries. Add restart: unless-stopped to the api service. Keep everything else the same.

For run_tests.sh — rewrite it properly with four clear steps. Step 1: check if the api container is running using docker compose ps, if it's not running start it with docker compose up -d --build, but also handle the case where it IS running but the API is not responding — in that case restart it. Step 2: wait for the API to be healthy by running wget inside the container via docker compose exec -T api wget -qO- against the health URL in a loop, print progress, and if it doesn't respond within 60 seconds print the last 30 lines of logs and exit with an error. Step 3: run unit tests inside the container using docker compose exec -T api npx jest --testPathPatterns=unit_tests --verbose --no-cache. Step 4: run API tests inside the container using docker compose exec -T api npx jest --testPathPatterns=API_tests --verbose --no-cache --runInBand. Capture the exit codes of both test runs separately so if one fails the other still runs. At the end print a clear summary showing which passed and which failed, and exit with a non-zero code if anything failed.

After making all three changes, run docker compose down, then 
run_tests.sh
 from scratch and confirm it starts the containers, waits for health, runs all tests inside the container, and prints a clean pass/fail summary.

{"file_path": "/home/someone/Documents/eagle-point/108/repo/run_tests.sh", "content": "#!/bin/sh

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 1 — Ensure containers are up
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
echo "=== Step 1: Ensuring containers are running ==="

api_running=$(docker compose ps --status running --format '{{.Service}}' 2>/dev/null | grep -c '^api$')

if [ "$api_running" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "API container is not running. Starting with build..."
  docker compose up -d --build
else
  # Container is running — verify it responds
  if ! docker compose exec -T api wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "API container is running but not responding. Restarting..."
    docker compose restart api
  else
    echo "API container is running and responding."
  fi
fi

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 2 — Wait for API health
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo "=== Step 2: Waiting for API to be healthy ==="

attempts=0
max_attempts=60

while [ "$attempts" -lt "$max_attempts" ]; do
  if docker compose exec -T api wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "API is healthy."
    break
  fi
  attempts=$((attempts + 1))
  printf "  waiting... (%d/%d)\n" "$attempts" "$max_attempts"
  sleep 1
done

if [ "$attempts" -ge "$max_attempts" ]; then
  echo ""
  echo "ERROR: API did not become healthy within ${max_attempts}s."
  echo "Last 30 lines of API logs:"
  docker compose logs api --tail 30
  exit 1
fi

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 3 — Unit tests
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo "=== Step 3: Running unit tests ==="

docker compose exec -T api npx jest --testPathPattern=unit_tests --verbose --no-cache
unit_exit=$?

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 4 — API tests
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo "=== Step 4: Running API tests ==="

docker compose exec -T api npx jest --testPathPattern=API_tests --verbose --no-cache --runInBand
api_exit=$?

# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Summary
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────
echo ""
echo "========================================"
echo "  TEST SUMMARY"
echo "========================================"

if [ "$unit_exit" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "  Unit tests:  PASSED"
else
  echo "  Unit tests:  FAILED (exit $unit_exit)"
fi

if [ "$api_exit" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "  API tests:   PASSED"
else
  echo "  API tests:   FAILED (exit $api_exit)"
fi

echo "========================================"

if [ "$unit_exit" -ne 0 ] || [ "$api_exit" -ne 0 ]; then
  exit 1
fi

exit 0
"}

Clean pass across the board. All 38 tests pass inside the container, clean summary at the end. Here's what changed:

**Dockerfile** — Single stage. `node:20-alpine`, install openssl, `npm ci`, copy everything (src, prisma, tests, config files), `npm run build`, symlink `dist/models/prisma` to the generated client, expose 3000, CMD node.

**docker-compose.yml** — `build: .` (no network hack), added `healthcheck` with wget on `/health` (10s interval, 15s start period, 5 retries), added `restart: unless-stopped`. Everything else unchanged.

**run_tests.sh** — Four clear steps: (1) check if api is running, start or restart as needed, (2) wait for health via `wget` inside the container with progress and 60s timeout, (3) unit tests with `--verbose --no-cache`, (4) API tests with `--verbose --no-cache --runInBand`. Captures exit codes independently, prints pass/fail summary, exits non-zero if anything failed.

Slice 1 and Slice 2 are done and tests pass. Now implement Slice 3 — RBAC and Authorization. Here's what needs to be built:

First update the Prisma schema to add Role, PermissionPoint, Menu, MenuPermissionPoint, RolePermissionPoint, and UserRole models. A Role has an id, name, and description. A PermissionPoint has an id and a code like "itinerary:read". A Menu is a logical capability bundle with a name and a list of permission points. UserRole links users to roles. Run a new Prisma migration for these models.

Then create 
rbac.service.ts
 with functions to: create a role, list roles, assign permission points to a role, create a permission point, list permission points, create a menu, assign roles to a user, and get a user's effective permission points by collecting all permission points from all their roles.

Update 
auth.middleware.ts
 to add a second middleware function called requirePermission(code: string) that checks if the authenticated user has a specific permission point — if not, return 403. Also add a requireRole(...roles: string[]) middleware that checks the user's role from the JWT payload.

Create 
users.controller.ts
 with endpoints: GET /users (admin only, list all users with pagination), GET /users/:id, PATCH /users/:id (update status), DELETE /users/:id (admin only). Create 
users.routes.ts
 and mount it in app.ts at /users.

Create 
rbac.controller.ts
 with endpoints: GET/POST /roles, POST /roles/:id/permissions, GET/POST /permission-points, GET/POST /menus, POST /users/:id/roles. Create 
rbac.routes.ts
 and mount at /roles, /permission-points, /menus.

Seed an admin user and an organizer user in 
seed.ts
 with the admin role having full permission points and organizer having itinerary-scoped ones.

For tests — add 
rbac.spec.ts
 testing: permission point assignment logic, role lookup, data-scope rule (organizer can only see own resources). Add 
rbac.api.spec.ts
 testing: create role 201, assign permissions 200, assign role to user 200, access protected route with correct role 200, access with wrong role 403, access without token 401.

After implementing, run 
run_tests.sh
 and confirm all Slice 1, 2, and 3 tests pass before moving to Slice 4.

1	# TripForge — Feature Build Order

3	Build one slice at a time. Each slice must be fully working (implementation + tests) before moving to the next.
4	
5	Use `docs/acceptance-checklist.md` as the gate tracker. Do not start a new slice until the current slice exit conditions are met.
6	
7	## Required implementation sequence (strict)
8	
9	1. Slice 1 (Foundation)
10	2. Slice 2 (Authentication and Identity)
11	3. Slice 3 (RBAC and Authorization)

15	7. Slice 7 (Share and Export)
16	8. Slice 8 (Data Import/Export)

20	12. Slice 12 (Final Polish)
21	13. Slice 13 (Delivery Acceptance Gate)

23	---
24	
25	## Slice 1 — Project Foundation
26	Done when:
27	- Express app boots with Prisma connected to MySQL
28	- `docker compose up` starts cleanly
29	- Prisma migrations run on startup
30	- Structured logging with request IDs on every request
31	- Health endpoint `GET /health` returns 200
32	- `.env.example` has all required vars
33	- `run_tests.sh` runs unit + integration tests
34	
35	Must pass before continuing:
36	- A local developer can run the project from README commands without editing code.
37	- At least one unit test and one integration test execute end-to-end (even if minimal bootstrap tests).
38	
39	---
40	
41	## Slice 2 — Authentication and Identity
42	Done when:
43	- `POST /auth/register` creates user with bcrypt password, stores security questions (AES-256 encrypted answers)
44	- `POST /auth/login` validates credentials, issues access token (30 min) + refresh token (14 days)
45	- `POST /auth/refresh` issues new access token from valid refresh token
46	- `POST /auth/logout` revokes refresh token
47	- `PATCH /auth/change-password` enforces last-5-reuse policy and complexity rules
48	- `POST /auth/recover` resets password via security question answers
49	- Device registration on login (max 5 active devices per user)
50	- Unusual-location detection: compare lastKnownCity to device.last_known_city → rate-limited challenge
51	- Account lockout after 10 failed attempts in 15 minutes
52	- Audit log on login, logout, password change, device events
53	- Unit tests: bcrypt, JWT, lockout, device limit, location detection
54	- Integration tests: login, wrong password, lockout, refresh, logout, recovery
55	
56	---
57	
58	## Slice 3 — RBAC and Authorization
59	Done when:
60	- Role CRUD (Admin only)
61	- Permission point CRUD (Admin only)
62	- Menu CRUD (logical capability bundles)
63	- Role-to-permission-point assignment
64	- User-to-role assignment
65	- RBAC middleware enforces permission points on all protected routes
66	- Data-scope rules: Organizer sees only own itineraries; Admin sees all
67	- Full audit trail on all permission changes
68	- Integration tests: role assignment, permission enforcement, cross-user 403
69	
70	---
71	
72	## Slice 4 — Resource Management
73	Done when:
74	- Resource CRUD (attraction, lodging, meal, meeting)
75	- Business hours CRUD per resource (day-of-week, open/close time)
76	- Closure dates CRUD per resource
77	- Travel time matrix CRUD (from/to resource, minutes, transport mode)
78	- Integration tests: CRUD, hours, closures, travel times
79	
80	---
81	
82	## Slice 5 — Itinerary Management
83	Done when:
84	- Itinerary CRUD (title, destination, dates, status)
85	- Add/update/remove items into day-based time slots
86	- Conflict validation:
87	  - Overlap detection (409)
88	  - 15-minute buffer enforcement (409)
89	  - Business hours check (400)
90	  - Closure date check (400)
91	  - Min dwell time check (400)

93	- Versioned revision on every save (snapshot + diff metadata)
94	- Version history endpoint

96	- Unit tests: overlap detection, buffer check, dwell time, travel time
97	- Integration tests: add item, conflict scenarios, version history

1	# TripForge — Feature Overview
2	
3	Offline-first backend API platform for travel itinerary planning, data ingestion, and model-assisted recommendations. Built with Express (TypeScript) + Prisma + MySQL. No UI. Pure backend.
4	
5	Implementation priority policy:
6	- P0: Security-critical controls (auth, authz, ownership, isolation, lockout, idempotency)
7	- P1: Core itinerary and resource workflows
8	- P2: Optimization, model decisioning, notifications, and operational polish
9	
10	Frozen clarification baseline (must be followed during implementation):
11	- Organizer scope is owner-only; admin is global.
12	- Refresh tokens are per-device.
13	- Device cap is strict at 5; 6th device login is rejected until removal.
14	- Unusual-city change triggers challenge with rate limit.
15	- Travel matrix is directed (non-symmetric).
16	- Missing business hours/closures means no restriction from that rule.
17	- Rules override model output in combined decisioning.
18	- Request ID is generated server-side when client omits `X-Request-Id`.
19	
20	Source of frozen decisions: `docs/questions.md`.
21	
22	---
23	
24	## Authentication and Identity
25	
26	What it does: Local username/password login with JWT, refresh tokens, device registration, and unusual-location detection.
27	
28	What needs to be built:
29	- Registration endpoint with bcrypt password hashing
30	- Login endpoint with JWT issuance (access token 30 min, refresh token 14 days)
31	- Refresh token endpoint
32	- Logout (revoke refresh token)
33	- Password change endpoint (enforces last-5-reuse policy)
34	- Password recovery via security questions (no email/SMS)
35	- Device registration (max 5 active devices per user)
36	- Unusual-location detection based on last-known city string from client
37	- Rate-limited challenge prompts on unusual location
38	- Account lockout after 10 failed attempts in 15 minutes
39	- Audit log on login, logout, password change, device registration
40	
41	---
42	
43	## RBAC and Authorization
44	
45	What it does: Role-based access control with permission points and menu groupings.
46	
47	What needs to be built:
48	- Role CRUD (Admin only)
49	- Permission point CRUD (Admin only)
50	- Menu CRUD — logical capability bundles grouping permission points
51	- Role-to-permission-point assignment
52	- User-to-role assignment
53	- API-level authorization middleware enforcing permission points
54	- Data-scope rules: Organizer sees only own itineraries; Admin sees all
55	- Full auditability of all permission changes
56	
57	---
58	
59	## Itinerary Management
60	
61	What it does: Create and manage travel itineraries with day-based time slots and conflict validation.
62	
63	What needs to be built:
64	- Itinerary CRUD (title, destination, start/end dates)
65	- Add/update/remove items into day-based time slots (day_number + start_time + end_time)
66	- Conflict validation:
67	  - Overlap detection between items on the same day
68	  - Minimum 15-minute buffer between items
69	  - Business hours enforcement per resource
70	  - Closure date enforcement per resource
71	  - Minimum dwell time enforcement per resource
72	  - Travel time matrix enforcement between consecutive items
73	- Versioned revision records on every save (with diff metadata)
74	- Share token generation (valid 7 days, same deployment only)
75	- Itinerary export (standardized package format)
76	- Version history endpoint
77	
78	---
79	
80	## Route Planning and Optimization
81	
82	What it does: Deterministic heuristic-based route optimization with explainable suggestions.
83	
84	What needs to be built:
85	- Same-area clustering (group items by city/region)
86	- Shortest-path approximation (nearest-neighbor algorithm)
87	- Ranked suggestion list (top 3 arrangements per day)
88	- Explainability payload per suggestion (reason string, estimated time saved)
89	- Travel time matrix CRUD (per city/region, per transport mode)
90	
91	---
92	
93	## Resource Management
94	
95	What it does: Manage attractions, lodging, meals, and meetings with business hours and closures.
96	
97	What needs to be built:
98	- Resource CRUD (name, type, location, min dwell time)
99	- Business hours CRUD per resource (day-of-week, open/close time)
100	- Closure dates CRUD per resource (date + reason)
101	- Travel time matrix entries between resources
102	
103	---
104	
105	## Data Import/Export
106	
107	What it does: Bulk import via Excel/CSV with pre-validation, deduplication, and rollback.
108	
109	What needs to be built:
110	- Excel/CSV template download per entity type
111	- Bulk upload endpoint (multipart form)
112	- Pre-validation pass: schema, required fields, type checks — returns row-level error annotations before commit
113	- Deduplication by configurable key (default: resource name + street_line + city)
114	- Commit endpoint to apply validated rows
115	- Rollback endpoint (available within 10-minute window per batch)
116	- Import batch status and error report endpoints
117	- Throughput target: 10,000 rows per batch
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Model Management and Inference
122	
123	What it does: Register, version, and run ML models locally with explainable inference.
124	
125	What needs to be built:
126	- Model registry CRUD (name, semantic version, type: PMML/ONNX/custom)
127	- Activation status management (inactive, active, canary)
128	- A/B allocation configuration (group name + percentage)
129	- Canary rollout management
130	- Inference endpoint: execute model locally via adapter
131	- Combined rule-and-model decisioning
132	- Explainability payload: top contributing features, confidence bands, applied rules
133	
134	---
135	
136	## Notification Center
137	
138	What it does: Fully local in-app notifications with templates, retry, and frequency caps.
139	
140	What needs to be built:
141	- Notification template CRUD (code, subject, body with {{variable}} placeholders)
142	- Send notification to user (resolves template variables)
143	- In-app notification list per user (with read/unread filter)
144	- Mark notification as read
145	- Delivery receipts and reach stats
146	- Retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff for failed deliveries
147	- Per-user frequency cap (default 20 messages/day)
148	- User blacklist flag
149	- Outbox pattern for reliable delivery
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Security
154	
155	What it does: Encryption, masking, strict password policy, immutable audit trail.
156	
157	What needs to be built:
158	- bcrypt password hashing (rounds=12)
159	- AES-256-GCM field-level encryption for security question answers and sensitive fields
160	- Strict password policy: 12+ chars, complexity (upper, lower, digit, special), last 5 reuse blocked
161	- Masking of sensitive fields in audit log exports
162	- Append-only audit_logs (no DELETE for app DB role)
163	- Idempotency keys for all mutating operations (stored 24 hours)
164	- Request IDs on every request (X-Request-Id header)
165	- Structured logging with sensitive field masking
166	
167	---
168	
169	## Observability
170	
171	What it does: Structured logs and database-backed job state for operational visibility.
172	
173	What needs to be built:
174	- Winston structured JSON logging on every request/response
175	- Database-backed job state for import batches and notification retries
176	- Import batch status endpoint
177	- Notification delivery stats endpoint
178
